cataloging_subject.label: Hoccleve's Works III: The Regement of Princes and Furnivall's fourteen-item appendix selection
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The exact intellectual/content unit delimited by the pinned U-M XML and Frederick J. Furnivall's 1897 EETS volume III: (1) fourteen separately headed verse items printed in the appendix on pp. xxiii-lxii, of which the first thirteen occur within the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul and the fourteenth is a separate complaint in Egerton MS 615; and (2) the complete printed Regement of Princes on pp. 1-197. It excludes the glossary on p. lxiii, all other front matter, the glossary and index on pp. 199-216, advertisements, and the broader three-volume Hoccleve series named in the electronic header. The cataloging unit is neither one medieval work nor a complete Hoccleve canon.

catalog_summary.title: Hoccleve's Works III: The Regement of Princes and Furnivall's fourteen-item appendix selection
catalog_summary.author: Thomas Hoccleve as author of The Regement of Princes; supported as translator of appendix item 7; attributed author in current DIMEV records for nine further appendix lyrics; responsibility for the remaining four Middle English realizations is unresolved
catalog_summary.date: The Regement composed 1410-1411; Middle English Pilgrimage carrier traditionally dated 1413; exact edited composite published 1897

titles.value: Hoccleve's Works III: The Regement of Princes and Furnivall's fourteen-item appendix selection
notes: The wording keeps the two-part electronic/edition boundary visible and does not imply uniform authorship across the appendix.

titles.value: Hoccleve's Works. III. The Regement of Princes A.D. 1411-12, from the Harleian MS. 4866, and Fourteen of Hoccleve's Minor Poems from the Egerton MS. 615
notes: Furnivall's title-page attribution is retained as an edition title, not adopted as a blanket modern authorship judgment.

titles.value: Hoccleve's works. Ed. by Frederick J. Furnivall.
notes: This broad U-M discovery title describes the cataloged three-volume set, while the encoded body in adq4048 is volume III only.

titles.value: Regement of Princes with fourteen-item appendix (adq4048)

agents.name: Thomas Hoccleve
notes: The XML header's 1370?-1450? dates are legacy catalog data; current scholarship generally places Hoccleve's death in 1426.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator of The Pilgrimage of the Soul
notes: This label preserves the anonymity of the larger Middle English translation and does not foreclose poem-level Hoccleve attributions.

agents.name: Henry Littlehales
notes: Furnivall credits Littlehales with copying most of the Egerton appendix material; the exact division of labor is retained at the edition layer.

agents.name: Guillaume de Deguileville
notes: Author of the French source work. This does not establish authorship or translation of any particular Middle English realization.

scope.description: The complete Regement of Princes, including its prologue, advice sections, and envoy
The lament of the green tree / Complaint of the Virgin, for which HM 111 preserves an incomplete holograph copy

notes: HM 111 lacks the first six stanzas but supplies strong authorial evidence for the surviving parallel portion; the poem renders a passage from Guillaume's French source.

scope.description: Nine English verse items assigned to Hoccleve by current DIMEV item records, apart from the securely supported Complaint of the Virgin

assertion.notes: DIMEV's item-level author fields are recorded as current catalog attributions, not converted into certainty about the English translator or the whole Pilgrimage.

scope.description: The anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage carrier context, not blanket responsibility for each embedded lyric

assertion.notes: This carrier-level responsibility does not assign the anonymous translator to any individual embedded lyric and coexists with poem-specific Hoccleve evidence.

responsibilities.display_name: Unresolved Middle English verse author or translator

scope.description: Items whose current DIMEV records point to Guillaume's French source authorship but do not explicitly establish responsibility for the Middle English realization

assertion.notes: Guillaume's source authorship is not treated as English authorship or translation responsibility.

scope.description: The 1897 edition, its exact composite boundary, ordering, supplied readings, notes, and H/R collation
Most appendix items collectively copied from Egerton MS 615 for Furnivall; the preface does not identify exactly which items

notes: Furnivall's preface credits Littlehales with copying most of the appendix material but does not supply item-level assignments. Furnivall's own transcription and rearrangement work is separately retained in the lineage record.
Furnivall's title says A.D. 1411-12; the current METS introduction dates composition to 1410-11. The difference is preserved rather than silently harmonized.

date_statements.display: Traditionally 1413 for the Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul

scope.description: Date of the English Pilgrimage carrier tradition, not an individual composition or translation date for any embedded lyric
Publication of the exact two-part edited composite cataloged here
Broad cultural origin of the Middle English works, not the production place of every witness or edition

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin rubrics, marginal source quotations, and brief French manuscript headings belong to embedded or transmission layers and do not displace Middle English as the primary language of the selected verse.

form_statements.description: Fifteen verse works totaling 6,716 intellectual verse lines in 959 direct stanza/verse-group elements.

assertion.notes: Raw descendant counts are 6,719 L and 960 LG because one Regement editorial note contains a nested three-line verse quotation and nested LG; those note elements are excluded from intellectual-text metrics.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, governance, counsel, and princely ethics
Poverty, patronage, clerical labor, and poetic writing
Virtues, vices, justice, mercy, prudence, and ethical conduct
War, peace, concord, and Christian rule
Salvation, penance, grace, death, judgment, and spiritual pilgrimage
Marian devotion, Christ's Passion, Nativity, and the liturgical year

summaries.value: This electronic/edition composite joins fourteen religious verse items from Egerton MS 615 to Thomas Hoccleve's Regement of Princes. The first thirteen appendix items are embedded lyrics from the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul; item 14 is a separate complaint, and item 7 has an incomplete Hoccleve holograph parallel. The Regement moves from an opening meditation and long dialogue with an old man into an address to Prince Henry and fifteen sections of political, ethical, and religious counsel, ending with a three-stanza envoy.

Included: appendix pp. xxiii-lxii and Regement pp. 1-197. Excluded: all other front matter, p. lxiii glossary, p. 198 boundary matter, glossary/index pp. 199-216, advertisements, volumes I-II, and the revised 1970 reprint. Historical titles and attributions remain edition-layer evidence rather than automatic whole-unit metadata.

extent.description: Fourteen appendix poems (179 direct LG, 1,253 L) plus the complete Regement (780 direct LG, 5,463 L). The 37 modeled content parts comprise the appendix container, a zero-count Pilgrimage-carrier grouping, fourteen poem children, the Regement parent, five major Regement divisions, and fifteen nested advice sections. Raw XML descendant counts are 960 LG and 6,719 L because an editorial note inside the largesse section embeds one LG and three quoted L elements; those are excluded from intellectual-text totals.

content_parts.label: Fourteen-item appendix selection from Egerton MS 615
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's appendix prints thirteen lyrics embedded in the Middle English Pilgrimage of the Soul followed by a separate complaint; manuscript order is not preserved and the first thirteen/items 1-13 must not be conflated with item 14.
notes: Furnivall prints manuscript item 14 last although it precedes the other selected passages in Egerton MS 615.

content_parts.label: Carrier context: thirteen verse passages in The Pilgrimage of the Soul
content_parts.summary: A zero-count structural grouping for appendix items 1-13 as passages embedded in the anonymous Middle English Pilgrimage carrier. It records carrier and translation context without assigning the carrier translator to each lyric.

locators.value: discontiguous embedded lyrics at fols. 17v-31v, 63r-67v, and 95v-102v

notes: Item-level authorship and translation responsibilities remain attached only where separately evidenced.

content_parts.label: The epistle of grace sent to the seek man. littera.
content_parts.summary: A 26-stanza, 182-line rhyme-royal letter of spiritual counsel spoken by Grace, printed as the first Pilgrimage lyric in the appendix.

content_parts.label: The chartre of pardon
content_parts.summary: A 14-stanza, 98-line rhyme-royal charter of pardon within the Middle English Pilgrimage, with English responsibility unresolved beyond the anonymous carrier translation.

content_parts.label: Cantus peregrinorum
content_parts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line Pilgrimage song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2065.

content_parts.label: Cantus angelorum
content_parts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line angels' song within the Middle English Pilgrimage; DIMEV links Guillaume as source author without thereby identifying the English versifier.

content_parts.label: The aungelys song within
content_parts.summary: A seven-stanza, 49-line angels' song within the Middle English Pilgrimage; French-source authorship and English realization remain distinct.

content_parts.label: The Angels' Song. Honour to Jesus.
content_parts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line devotional song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2064.

content_parts.label: A lamentacioun of the grene tree, complaynyng of the losyng of hire appill
content_parts.summary: A forty-stanza, 280-line Marian lament, now commonly treated as Hoccleve's Complaint of the Virgin; HM 111 preserves a holograph parallel beginning with stanza 7.

locators.value: 3r-7v; incomplete parallel lacking the first six stanzas

content_parts.label: The recordyng of aungeles song of the Natiuite of oure lady
content_parts.summary: A seven-stanza, 49-line song for the Nativity of the Virgin assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2063.

content_parts.label: The aungeles song
content_parts.summary: A two-stanza, 14-line angels' song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2061.

content_parts.label: The aungeles songe in the feste of the Epiphanie of oure lord
content_parts.summary: A four-stanza, 28-line Epiphany song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2060.

content_parts.label: The aungeles song on pask day
content_parts.summary: A five-stanza, 35-line Easter song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2067.

content_parts.label: The song of graces of alle seintes upon Paske day
content_parts.summary: A four-stanza, 28-line Easter song of the saints assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2062.

content_parts.label: The aungeles song & alle othir seintes in the feste of Pentecost
content_parts.summary: A three-stanza, 21-line Pentecost song assigned to Hoccleve by DIMEV record 2066.

content_parts.label: The pietous complainte of the Soule
content_parts.summary: A 52-stanza, 364-line complaint copied from an earlier, separate Egerton passage and printed last by Furnivall; it is not one of the thirteen embedded Pilgrimage lyrics grouped by his appendix heading.

content_parts.label: The Regement of Princes
content_parts.summary: Hoccleve's 5,463-line rhyme-royal mirror for Prince Henry combines an autobiographical opening and dialogue about poverty and patronage with a formal address, fifteen sections of princely counsel, and an envoy to the book.

content_parts.label: Opening meditation
content_parts.summary: Hoccleve reflects on worldly instability, poverty, thought, and writing before the encounter with the old man.

content_parts.label: Meeting and dialogue with an old man
content_parts.summary: An old man questions and counsels Hoccleve about poverty, annuity, service, writing, and seeking princely patronage.
notes: The XML repeats Furnivall's heading 'old beggar'; the current METS edition describes the interlocutor as an old man, so the normalized label avoids making beggar status a settled identity.

content_parts.label: Address and proem to Prince Henry
content_parts.summary: Hoccleve addresses the prince, presents his book, invokes Chaucer and named authorities, and turns from the dialogue toward formal counsel.
notes: The first five stanzas, lines 2017-2051, are supplied in Furnivall from Royal MS 17 D VI because Harley 4866 lacks the presentation leaf.

content_parts.label: Fifteen sections of princely counsel
content_parts.summary: The main advice sequence treats royal dignity and oaths, justice, law, pity, mercy, patience, chastity, magnanimity, riches, largesse, avarice, prudence, counsel, and peace.

content_parts.label: On the dignity of a king
content_parts.summary: The first counsel section defines royal dignity and the obligations attached to it.

content_parts.label: On coronation oaths, truth, and cautious speech
content_parts.summary: Counsel on a king's oaths, truthfulness, promises, and disciplined speech.

content_parts.label: Of justice
content_parts.summary: Counsel on impartial justice and the harms caused by its corruption.

content_parts.label: On observing the laws
content_parts.summary: Counsel that rulers honor and uphold the laws by which their people are governed.

content_parts.label: Of pity
content_parts.summary: Counsel on pity as compassionate assistance and a royal virtue.

content_parts.label: Of mercy
content_parts.summary: Counsel on mercy, forgiveness, and relief of another's suffering.

content_parts.label: Of patience
content_parts.summary: Counsel on patience, endurance, charity, and mastery of anger.

content_parts.label: Of chastity
content_parts.summary: Counsel on chastity and resistance to sexual vice.

content_parts.label: Of royal magnanimity
content_parts.summary: Counsel on magnanimity, honor, and the prince's proper estimation of worldly rank.

content_parts.label: That a king should not place happiness in riches
content_parts.summary: Counsel against treating wealth as the source of royal happiness.

content_parts.label: Of largesse and prodigality
content_parts.summary: Counsel distinguishing virtuous largesse from wasteful prodigality.
notes: A nested editorial note contains a three-line quotation encoded as LG/L; those note elements are excluded from this part's metrics.

content_parts.label: Of avarice
content_parts.summary: Counsel against avarice and insatiable accumulation.

content_parts.label: Of royal prudence
content_parts.summary: Counsel on prudence, foresight, and discerning governance.

content_parts.label: On taking counsel in all actions
content_parts.summary: Counsel on selecting advisers and seeking deliberation before action.

content_parts.label: Of peace
content_parts.summary: Counsel on internal and international peace, Christian concord, war, and the duties of rulers.

content_parts.label: The envoy
content_parts.summary: Three eight-line stanzas address the book as it approaches Prince Henry and ask that its plain style and faults be excused.
notes: Furnivall supplies the edition's envoy from Royal MS 17 D VI because Harley 4866 has lost its last leaf; HM 111 is a separate holograph parallel, not the printed base for the edition's supplied passage.

related_works.title: The Pilgrimage of the Soul
notes: Appendix items 1-13 are verse passages embedded in this anonymous Middle English translation; appendix item 14 is modeled separately.

related_works.title: Le Pèlerinage de l'âme
notes: Guillaume de Deguileville authored the French source work. This relation does not name the Middle English translator or settle the authorship of each English lyric.

related_works.title: De regimine principum
notes: Giles of Rome is an important named source, but Hoccleve's Regement is a composite work of counsel with additional sources and original framing rather than a simple one-source rendering.

evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY DIV1 nodes :3 and :4; all descendant DIV2/DIV3/HEAD/LG/L/PB/MILESTONE elements
evidence.summary: Supplies the exact electronic boundary, edition title page, fourteen appendix headings, Regement structure and text, page sequence, editorial notes, source markers, and historical U-M encoding declaration.

evidence.citation: Deterministic element, boundary, stanza, line, page, and nesting audit of the pinned adq4048 XML.
evidence.locator: Included DIV1 nodes ADQ4048.0001.001:3-4; direct verse LG/L contrasted with all descendant LG/L
evidence.summary: Confirms fourteen appendix items at SEQ 25-65/pp. xxiii-lxii (179 direct LG, 1,253 L), the Regement at SEQ 66-263/pp. 1-197 (780 direct LG, 5,463 L), all part metrics, and exclusion of p. lxiii and back matter. Raw descendant counts add one editorial-note LG and three editorial-note L elements.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Hoccleve's works, ADQ4048.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current public item, title, publication statement, digital text and page views, and current No Copyright-United States designation; the exact edition boundary is checked against the XML rather than inferred from the broad item header.

evidence.citation: adq4048 lineage evidence for Furnivall's 1897 volume III and complete BSB facsimile.
evidence.summary: Supports the exact title, publication, two-part selection, pages/canvases, ordering, editorial roles, Harley/Royal source distinctions, and omitted matter.

evidence.citation: adq4048 lineage evidence for British Library Egerton MS 615 and Furnivall's source mapping.
evidence.summary: Supports the single-codex source, manuscript date and origin, Pilgrimage boundary, fourteen discontiguous poem loci, and current image-access limitations.

evidence.citation: adq4048 lineage evidence for Harley MS 4866, Royal MS 17 D VI, Furnivall's preface, and source collation.
evidence.summary: Supports Harley as the base witness, Royal as continuous collate and direct supplier of the missing presentation block and envoy, and the distinction between later replacement writing and medieval source text.

evidence.citation: adq4048 lineage evidence for Huntington MS HM 111, its complete IIIF facsimile, and pinned Hoccleve Archive transcriptions.
evidence.summary: Supports Hoccleve's holograph status, the incomplete Complaint of the Virgin at fols. 3r-7v, the Regement envoy at fols. 39v-40r, exact IIIF mappings, and separate archive transcription layers.

Supports item-level DIMEV identifiers and current responsibility fields while explicitly distinguishing Guillaume's French-source authorship from authorship or translation of the Middle English realizations.

evidence.citation: Charles R. Blyth, ed., The Regiment of Princes, current Middle English Texts Series introduction and edition.
evidence.locator: Introduction: poem, poet, context, sources; manuscripts; previous editions
evidence.summary: Supports Hoccleve's authorship, 1410-11 composition, Prince Henry dedication, mirror-for-princes context, manuscript census, Harley/Royal/Furnivall relationships, and the current description of the interlocutor as an old man.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, Thomas Hoccleve, The Regement of Princes.
evidence.summary: Confirms Hoccleve attribution, 1411 date, Prince Henry dedication, Middle English verse form, Furnivall 1897 edition and 1997 reprint, and modern edition bibliography.

evidence.citation: Jennifer E. Bryan, 'Hoccleve, the Virgin, and the Politics of Complaint,' PMLA 135.5 (2020), with HM 111 and source evidence recorded in the lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Supports current treatment of the Complaint of the Virgin as a Hoccleve work related to Guillaume de Deguileville's French source; manuscript extent and incompleteness are independently established from HM 111.

open_questions.question: Which of the thirteen English Pilgrimage lyrics besides the Complaint of the Virgin can be assigned to Hoccleve, and on what poem-specific evidence?
next_steps: Review DIMEV's cited bibliography and current specialist studies item by item.
Keep the anonymous carrier translator, DIMEV catalog attributions, and Guillaume's French-source authorship as distinct assertions.

open_questions.question: What is the precise source, authorship, and relationship of The pietous complainte of the Soule to the Middle English Pilgrimage carrier?
next_steps: Inspect current scholarship and DIMEV record 878's sources without treating Guillaume's source field as an English authorship claim.
Preserve the separate Egerton locus and Furnivall's rearranged printed order.

open_questions.question: Can direct manuscript inspection refine which exact Regement readings come from Harley's medieval hand, its replacement leaves, Royal 17 D VI, and Furnivall's editorial intervention?
next_steps: Request current British Library images or onsite consultation of Harley 4866 and Royal 17 D VI.
Retain Furnivall's explicit supplied blocks and continuous H/R apparatus meanwhile.

open_questions.question: What exact physical copy, OCR workflow, and item-specific selection decision produced the 2006 U-M encoding and historical 292-image layer?
next_steps: Seek item-level U-M production records and scan provenance.
Do not infer the scanned copy from the BSB or Google carriers.

notes: Content-part titles retain Furnivall/XML wording where it identifies exact items; interpretive normalization is confined to catalog labels and summaries.
The title-page phrase 'fourteen of Hoccleve's minor poems' is historical edition evidence. It is not used to erase item-level authorship uncertainty.
Guillaume de Deguileville is modeled as author of a French source work, never automatically as author or translator of a Middle English realization.
The Regement's editorially supplied and collated witness relationships belong to lineage; work metadata records their consequence only where it affects exact content boundaries.
Furnivall's p. lxiii glossary and all material after the Regement on p. 197 are outside the cataloging subject even though they remain in complete cached carriers.
